data.description:
## Reproduction Steps

1. Dispatch reconcile-bridge-canary.yml (dry_run=false) so it writes a heartbeat ticket event, or observe a scheduled reconcile-bridge run that writes inbound Jira events.
2. Fetch the tickets branch and inspect the created event JSON.

## Expected vs Actual

- **Expected:** the event carries author_sig (signed by the Rebar Bot identity 594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d), verifiable via rebar verify-authorship.
- **Actual:** the event is ATTRIBUTED (author_id/author_email = bot) but has NO author_sig. No 'could not sign' warning appears, so signing was never attempted — the write step's rebar config never saw identity.signing_key.

## Root cause

Stories 1787 (canary) and 9e76 (bridge) materialize secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY to a 0600 $RUNNER_TEMP file and export REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY via $GITHUB_ENV in an early step. That cross-step $GITHUB_ENV value is not taking effect in the later rebar write steps (REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY appears 0 times in post-materialize step env in the run log). So attribution (git-email resolution) works but signing does not.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] reconcile-bridge.yml AND reconcile-bridge-canary.yml set REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY at the JOB-level env (static ${{ runner.temp }}/rebar-bot-signing-key path) so every write step sees it, instead of relying on cross-step $GITHUB_ENV propagation.
- [ ] A dispatched canary run (dry_run=false) produces a ticket event whose author_sig verifies as the bot identity via rebar verify-authorship.
- [ ] make lint && make typecheck && make test pass.
data.title: CI ticket writes attributed but UNSIGNED — job-level env for REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY
